title: Schizophrenia: Community Care Coordination and Relapse Prevention
subtitle: A 28-year-old man with schizophrenia comes to the community clinic with early warning signs after missing doses for two weeks. The nurse coordinates care — adherence support, a long-acting injectable, interdisciplinary follow-up — to prevent relapse.

patient.name: Elijah Brooks
patient.admitDate: Community mental health clinic. Established schizophrenia, previously stable on a second-generation antipsychotic. Missed doses for ~2 weeks with early warning signs (sleep disturbance, social withdrawal, rising suspiciousness). Weight up; due for metabolic monitoring.

labs.v: Psychiatry, case mgmt, PCP, social work.

nursesNote: Elijah Brooks, 28, schizophrenia, previously stable, now with early relapse warning signs after ~2 weeks of missed doses; weight up and due for metabolic monitoring. Schizophrenia needs coordinated, continuous care; medication adherence is the strongest relapse-prevention factor. Plan: assess symptoms/adherence nonjudgmentally, address barriers and consider a long-acting injectable, coordinate the interdisciplinary team (psychiatry, case management, primary care, therapy, social work), provide patient/family psychoeducation on warning signs, arrange metabolic monitoring, and ensure follow-up with a warm hand-off.
providerOrders: Assess symptoms, adherence, and relapse warning signs
Address adherence barriers; consider a long-acting injectable antipsychotic
Coordinate care with case management, primary care, therapy, and social work
Provide patient/family psychoeducation and a relapse-prevention plan
Arrange metabolic monitoring and follow-up with a warm hand-off

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does this patient's care require?

opts.text: Schizophrenia is a chronic illness needing coordinated, continuous care
Medication adherence is the strongest factor in preventing relapse
Early relapse signs include sleep changes, withdrawal, and rising suspiciousness
Long-acting injectable antipsychotics can improve adherence
Once stable, the patient no longer needs follow-up or monitoring
People with serious mental illness have higher rates of untreated physical illness
Nonadherence is a leading cause of relapse and rehospitalization
Addressing barriers (side effects, cost, insight, transport) supports adherence
Antipsychotics require metabolic monitoring (weight, glucose, lipids)
Family psychoeducation and reduced expressed emotion help prevent relapse
Confidential information may be shared freely with anyone who asks
Care should be coordinated across psychiatry, case management, and primary care
Assess current symptoms, adherence, and any early warning signs
Address adherence barriers and consider a long-acting injectable
Coordinate the interdisciplinary team (case manager, PCP, therapist, social work)
Educate the patient and family on warning signs and the plan
Ensure follow-up, monitoring, and a warm hand-off; document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action support or undermine the care plan?

opts.text: Coordinate a long-acting injectable to support adherence
Schedule follow-up and give a warm hand-off to the case manager
Teach the patient and family early relapse warning signs
Arrange metabolic monitoring with the primary-care provider
Address barriers to adherence (side effects, cost, transport)
Use a nonjudgmental, collaborative approach
Dismiss missed appointments as the patient "not caring"
Ignore early warning signs until a full relapse occurs
Share clinical details without the patient's consent

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Build rapport and assess adherence nonjudgmentally
Problem-solve barriers and arrange a long-acting injectable if appropriate
Connect the patient to case management and primary care
Provide family psychoeducation and a relapse-prevention plan
Schedule follow-up with a warm hand-off and document the plan
Adherence improves with a long-acting injectable and symptoms stay controlled
The patient attends follow-up and engages with the case manager
Early warning signs are caught and treatment is adjusted promptly
Metabolic monitoring and primary care keep physical health on track
Missed doses lead to returning hallucinations and rehospitalization
Early warning signs are ignored until a full psychotic relapse
Fragmented, siloed care causes a gap and the patient is lost to follow-up
Untreated metabolic side effects lead to new diabetes

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
